longTailKeywords: color vision loss after eye disease

h1Title: Acquired Color Vision Deficiency: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Acquired color vision deficiency is a reduction or loss of color discrimination that develops during a person's lifetime as a consequence of ocular or systemic disease, drug toxicity, or neurological injury — distinct from the congenital X-linked color blindness present from birth. Unlike congenital defects, which almost always follow a red-green pattern, acquired defects most commonly affect blue-yellow discrimination (tritan axis), though red-green and diffuse losses occur depending on the underlying cause. The condition affects an estimated 1-5% of patients with significant retinal or optic nerve disease and can be an early, sensitive marker of progressive damage in conditions such as glaucoma, macular degeneration, and diabetic retinopathy. Management targets the underlying cause; the color vision loss itself may be partially or fully reversible when the trigger is removed or treated.

keyStats.prevalence: Acquired color vision loss affects an estimated 8% of patients with open-angle glaucoma and 10-25% with early diabetic macular edema (Ramirez & Bhatt, Br J Ophthalmol 2019); precise population prevalence is not separately reported
keyStats.demographics: Equally affects men and women (unlike congenital red-green deficiency, which predominantly affects men); more prevalent in older adults with age-related ocular disease
keyStats.avgAge: Most cases arise in adults over 40 due to the higher prevalence of underlying retinal, optic nerve, and vascular disease in that age group
keyStats.globalCases: No isolated global case count; occurs as part of the broader burden of glaucoma (80 million), age-related macular degeneration (200 million), and diabetic retinopathy (100 million) worldwide

definition: Acquired color vision deficiency (ICD-10: H53.52), also called acquired dyschromatopsia, is a deterioration in the ability to distinguish colors that arises secondary to pathology in the retina, optic nerve, visual cortex, or as a result of systemic disease or medication toxicity. The condition is classified along two axes: Type I (red-green, protan-deutan axis), most common with optic nerve disease; Type II (blue-yellow, tritan axis), the earliest color axis to fail in retinal diseases such as age-related macular degeneration and diabetic retinopathy; and Type III (diffuse or achromatopsia-like), seen in severe bilateral disease or toxic optic neuropathy. The underlying mechanism differs depending on the anatomical site: macular photoreceptor loss selectively disrupts short-wave (blue) cone function earliest; optic nerve disease impairs red-green discrimination; cortical lesions may produce color agnosia. Acquired deficiency differs from congenital in that it is usually unilateral or asymmetric, monocular comparison is informative, the defect may progress or regress, and it often identifies otherwise subclinical disease. Ophthalmologists, neurologists, and toxicologists all encounter acquired color vision deficiency depending on the underlying cause.

typesClassification.type: Type I (red-green / protan-deutan axis) acquired defect
typesClassification.description: Predominantly caused by optic neuropathy — glaucoma, optic neuritis, ischemic optic neuropathy, compressive optic nerve lesions. Patients confuse reds and greens and report muted or washed-out red saturation. Identified on Ishihara plates and confirmed on Farnsworth-Munsell 100-hue testing.

typesClassification.type: Type II (blue-yellow / tritan axis) acquired defect
typesClassification.description: The hallmark of macular and outer retinal disease: age-related macular degeneration, central serous chorioretinopathy, diabetic macular edema, hydroxychloroquine toxicity, and cone dystrophies. Patients confuse blues with greens and yellows with whites. Detected by Panel D-15, FM100-hue, or color contrast sensitivity tests.

Severe, widespread color loss affecting all axes; seen in advanced bilateral optic atrophy, toxic optic neuropathy (methanol, ethambutol, cisplatin), severe vitamin B12 deficiency optic neuropathy, and cerebral achromatopsia from occipital cortex lesions.

Specific medications — ethambutol (tuberculosis), hydroxychloroquine (rheumatoid arthritis, lupus), sildenafil (phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors), digitalis, chloroquine, and high-dose amiodarone — selectively damage the retinal pigment epithelium or optic nerve to produce color discrimination loss as an early warning sign of toxicity.

primarySymptoms: Difficulty distinguishing colors that previously appeared clearly different — commonly blues from greens, or reds from oranges, depending on the axis affected.
Colors appearing 'washed out', less saturated, or generally duller than normal in one or both eyes.
Asymmetric color perception between the two eyes — noticing that the same object looks a different shade in each eye when tested separately.
Reduced ability to match colors accurately while painting, selecting clothing, working in color-critical professions, or interpreting colored warning signs.
Difficulty reading color-coded charts, maps, electrical wiring, or laboratory staining slides that were previously easily interpreted.
Impaired color contrast — difficulty separating similar colors in low-contrast environments such as dim lighting or foggy conditions.
Persistent or progressive changes in color perception associated with other visual symptoms (central blur, scotoma, field loss) pointing to retinal or optic nerve pathology.
Blue-yellow confusion as an early asymptomatic finding discovered on routine ophthalmologic screening for glaucoma or macular disease before the patient notices any change.
Loss of the ability to detect the redness of an optic disc or retinal hemorrhage — recognized by ophthalmologists as a sign of optic nerve or retinal disease rather than reported by patients.
earlyWarningSigns: Noticing that traffic lights or the colors of familiar objects look subtly different between the two eyes when each is covered and tested alternately.
Difficulty passing a color discrimination test required for a professional license (pilot, driver, electrician) that was previously passed — the test may be more sensitive than daily life.
A patient starting ethambutol, hydroxychloroquine, chloroquine, or high-dose amiodarone who notices any new difficulty with colors, even minor, before other visual symptoms appear.
Routine color vision testing showing a shift toward the tritan axis in a patient with early diabetic macular changes or suspected macular degeneration.
emergencySigns: Sudden complete loss of color vision in one eye combined with acute visual loss — suggests central retinal artery occlusion or anterior ischemic optic neuropathy requiring same-day vascular evaluation.
Acute bilateral color vision loss with visual field defects and headache — suggests toxic optic neuropathy from methanol ingestion or other optic nerve emergency.
Profound rapid color vision loss in a patient taking ethambutol, signaling onset of ethambutol optic neuropathy — the drug must be stopped the same day to prevent irreversible damage.
Color vision loss plus pericentral scotoma in a patient on hydroxychloroquine — early sign of irreversible hydroxychloroquine retinopathy; ophthalmology review within days.

causes.description: Progressive optic nerve fiber loss in open-angle glaucoma reduces red-green discrimination, typically as a Type I acquired defect. Color vision loss correlates with visual field damage and can precede detectable field loss on standard perimetry. Ischemic optic neuropathy (anterior or posterior) causes sudden and often profound color defects.

causes.cause: Age-related macular degeneration and diabetic retinopathy
causes.description: Degeneration of macular cone photoreceptors — especially short-wave (blue) cones, which are most vulnerable to metabolic insult — produces Type II tritan-axis defects. Diabetic macular edema and hard exudates disrupt outer retinal function before acuity falls significantly, making color vision a sensitive early functional marker.

Ethambutol chelates zinc in the optic nerve and produces dose-dependent red-green color loss; risk rises above 15 mg/kg/day and cumulative duration over 2 months. Hydroxychloroquine and chloroquine deposit in the retinal pigment epithelium and produce parafoveal cone damage; a tritan defect is among the earliest detected findings on macular screening. Phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors transiently alter blue-yellow discrimination through cyclic-GMP-mediated cone effects.

Demyelinating optic neuritis reduces red-green discrimination in the affected eye, often producing red desaturation (the red lens phenomenon) that persists even after visual acuity recovers to normal. This residual color defect is a sensitive marker of prior optic nerve damage and is routinely used in neurology assessment.

Vitamin B12 deficiency, folate deficiency, copper deficiency, and thiamine deficiency impair optic nerve function through mitochondrial and myelin mechanisms, producing a progressive Type I or III defect. Methanol, tobacco-alcohol amblyopia, and heavy metals (lead, mercury) cause toxic optic neuropathies with prominent color vision loss.

Bilateral lesions of the lingual and fusiform gyri (areas V4) from stroke, trauma, or posterior cortical atrophy cause loss of color perception despite structurally intact retinas and optic nerves. Cortical color blindness (achromatopsia) is rare but represents a distinct acquired form diagnosed by neuroimaging.

riskFactors.description: Open-angle glaucoma causes progressive optic nerve damage that preferentially impairs color discrimination. Color vision loss detectable in 8-15% of glaucoma patients even with near-normal visual acuity.
Both dry and wet AMD disrupt macular photoreceptors; blue-yellow discrimination loss is among the earliest detectable functional changes, preceding acuity loss by months in some patients.
Diabetic macular edema reduces color contrast sensitivity before clinical acuity drops. Better glycaemic and blood pressure control slows macular progression and preserves color function.

riskFactors.factor: Use of ethambutol, hydroxychloroquine, or chloroquine
riskFactors.description: These medications are used long-term for tuberculosis and autoimmune diseases; baseline color testing before initiating therapy and 6-monthly monitoring is standard of care. Risk is dose- and duration-dependent.
A first episode of optic neuritis leaves residual color deficits in 80% of patients even after acuity recovery; MS patients with recurrent attacks accumulate progressive optic nerve damage.
Deficiencies produce optic neuropathy that is usually reversible if caught early; risk factors include veganism, bariatric surgery, chronic antacid use, and alcoholism.

riskFactors.factor: Occupational exposure to heavy metals or organic solvents
riskFactors.description: Workers exposed to lead, mercury, styrene, carbon disulfide, or toluene have higher rates of acquired color vision deficiency in occupational health cohorts; annual color monitoring is recommended in high-exposure industries.

affectedDemographics: Older adults over 50 years bear the highest burden due to the prevalence of glaucoma, macular degeneration, and diabetic retinopathy in this age group.
Men and women are equally susceptible to acquired defects — unlike congenital red-green color blindness, which affects approximately 8% of men and 0.5% of women.
Patients with long-standing type 1 or type 2 diabetes are at elevated risk; diabetic retinopathy affects approximately 34% of diabetic individuals worldwide.
TB patients receiving ethambutol for multi-drug regimens require monitoring; color vision loss occurs in 1-6% depending on dose.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of acquired color vision deficiency begins with careful history — which colors are confused, whether onset was sudden or gradual, laterality, drug history, systemic diseases, and occupational exposures. Monocular comparison under the same lighting condition is the most sensitive patient-reported test and is checked by asking the patient to compare the apparent color of a red object seen with each eye separately. Formal testing uses pseudoisochromatic plates (Ishihara, Hardy-Rand-Rittler) as a screening tool, but the Ishihara plates are designed primarily for congenital red-green defects and miss tritan (blue-yellow) acquired defects. The Farnsworth-Munsell 100-Hue test and its abbreviated Form D-15 are the standards for characterizing the axis of confusion and severity. The Lanthony Desaturated D-15 (D-15d) is more sensitive for mild losses. Once a defect is characterized, the ophthalmologist directs investigation toward the anatomical cause: visual field perimetry, optical coherence tomography (OCT) of the macula and retinal nerve fiber layer, color-contrast sensitivity testing, electrophysiology (pattern ERG, multifocal ERG, VEP), and fundus photography. Neuroimaging (MRI brain with gadolinium) is added when cortical or optic nerve compression is suspected. Drug levels and nutritional blood tests (serum B12, copper, folate) are relevant in specific scenarios.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screen for color axis confusion; Ishihara primarily screens red-green axis; HRR detects both red-green and tritan defects
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient reads numbers or traces paths on colored-dot plates under standardized daylight illumination at 75 cm. Takes 5 minutes. Identifies confusion axis but cannot quantify severity or distinguish acquired from congenital.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Characterizes the axis and severity of color confusion; standard for monitoring disease progression and drug toxicity
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient arranges a series of colored caps in order under bright illumination. FM100-Hue takes 15-20 minutes; D-15d takes 5 minutes. The error score and axis of the confusion plot distinguish tritan, protan-deutan, and diffuse defects.

diagnosticTests.test: Optical coherence tomography (OCT) of macula and RNFL
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects structural retinal or optic nerve damage underlying the color loss — macular thinning, drusen, edema, RNFL loss from glaucoma
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Non-contact scan using a laser. Patient fixates a target for 10-15 seconds per eye. No discomfort. Provides cross-sectional and thickness maps instantly.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects the pattern of visual field loss that corresponds to optic nerve damage in glaucoma or optic neuropathy
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient presses a button whenever they see a small light against a white background inside a bowl-shaped instrument. Takes 5-8 minutes per eye. Requires concentration; learning effect improves reliability with repetition.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies macular photoreceptor and ganglion cell function and optic nerve conduction — detects functional loss before structural changes are visible
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Small electrodes placed near the eye (ERG) or on the scalp (VEP); patient views a reversing checkerboard pattern. Takes 30-60 minutes. Useful for drug toxicity monitoring and diagnosing optic nerve disease.

diagnosticTests.test: MRI brain and orbit with gadolinium
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes compressive or demyelinating optic nerve disease and cortical dyschromatopsia from occipital lesions
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 45-60 minutes in a standard MRI scanner; contrast injection via a cannula. Essential when color loss is associated with other neurological signs, afferent pupil defect, or suspected MS.

treatmentOverview: There is no drug or surgical treatment that directly restores lost color discrimination; management is directed entirely at the underlying cause. For drug-induced color vision deficiency, the most important intervention is stopping or reducing the offending agent before irreversible damage occurs. Ethambutol optic neuropathy can recover substantially — and sometimes completely — if the drug is stopped early (within weeks of onset); delayed cessation produces permanent defects. Hydroxychloroquine retinopathy is generally irreversible once the bull's-eye maculopathy stage is reached, which is why the American Academy of Ophthalmology 2016 hydroxychloroquine screening guidelines mandate baseline and annual screening so that toxicity is caught at its earliest, asymptomatic stage (10-micron OCT thinning or multifocal ERG signal loss). For glaucoma-related color loss, treating IOP to target with drops, laser, or surgery arrests further optic nerve damage and stabilizes color discrimination. For optic neuritis, high-dose intravenous methylprednisolone (1 g/day for 3 days) accelerates acuity recovery but does not improve the ultimate degree of color vision recovery at 6-12 months compared with placebo in the Optic Neuritis Treatment Trial (ONTT). In diabetic retinopathy with macular edema, anti-VEGF injections (ranibizumab, bevacizumab, aflibercept) and photocoagulation reduce macular edema and improve color contrast sensitivity in parallel with acuity. For nutritional optic neuropathy, supplementation of the deficient nutrient — intramuscular vitamin B12, oral copper — halts progression and often produces meaningful recovery. Visual rehabilitation aids including color-enhancing tinted lenses (such as ChromaGen or X-Chrom tints) and smartphone color-identification apps (e.g., Color Blind Pal, Be My Eyes) help patients compensate for persistent defects in occupational and daily-life settings.

medicalTreatments.name: Discontinuation or dose reduction of the causative drug
medicalTreatments.description: For ethambutol, hydroxychloroquine, chloroquine, or other toxic agents: stopping the drug as soon as color vision loss is detected is the single most effective intervention. Early cessation (within 4 weeks of onset for ethambutol) is associated with recovery in 50-80% of cases.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Recovery of ethambutol color vision in 50-80% if stopped within 4-8 weeks of onset; hydroxychloroquine maculopathy is largely irreversible once structural damage is visible on OCT

medicalTreatments.name: Intraocular pressure-lowering therapy (topical drops, laser, surgery) for glaucoma
medicalTreatments.description: Prostaglandin analogs (latanoprost, bimatoprost), beta-blockers (timolol), and carbonic anhydrase inhibitors reduce IOP to preserve remaining optic nerve fibers. Selective laser trabeculoplasty (SLT) and trabeculectomy are used for inadequate drug response.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Each 1 mmHg IOP reduction reduces glaucoma progression rate by approximately 10%; color vision loss stabilizes when IOP is controlled to target

medicalTreatments.name: Anti-VEGF intravitreal injections (ranibizumab, aflibercept, bevacizumab) for diabetic macular edema
medicalTreatments.description: Monthly or treat-and-extend anti-VEGF injections reduce macular edema, improve photoreceptor layer thickness on OCT, and improve color contrast sensitivity alongside acuity in diabetic patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Color contrast sensitivity improves in parallel with acuity gains in 35-50% of treated patients in randomized trials of anti-VEGF versus laser

medicalTreatments.name: Vitamin B12 intramuscular supplementation (1 mg cyanocobalamin weekly until repleted)
medicalTreatments.description: For B12-deficiency optic neuropathy: normalizes serum B12, halts optic nerve demyelination, and produces color vision recovery in 40-60% of treated patients if started within 6 months of symptom onset.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Partial or full color vision recovery in 40-60% of early cases; long-standing deficiency with optic atrophy is less likely to recover

medicalTreatments.name: Color-enhancing spectacle tints (ChromaGen, X-Chrom lenses)
medicalTreatments.description: Specialized tinted contact or spectacle lenses alter wavelength sensitivity to enhance color contrast for specific defects. Clinical benefit is variable and individual; NHS and some insurance systems provide these for occupational needs.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improved Ishihara pass rate in 63-80% of users in small trials; does not restore true color discrimination but improves practical color task performance

alternativeTreatments.name: Smartphone color-identification apps
alternativeTreatments.description: Applications such as Color Blind Pal, Microsoft Seeing AI, and Colorblind Avenger use the phone camera and AI to identify and name colors in real time. Validated in occupational and daily-living contexts as a practical compensation tool.

whySeeSpecialist: Acquired color vision deficiency is often the earliest detectable sign of treatable underlying disease — glaucoma, drug toxicity, diabetic macular edema — before acuity or visual fields change. An ophthalmologist can characterize the defect axis, identify the anatomical cause with imaging, and direct time-sensitive interventions such as stopping ethambutol or beginning IOP-lowering therapy. Neuroimaging referral is needed when optic nerve compression or cortical disease is possible.
doctorSelectionGuide: A comprehensive ophthalmologist or neuro-ophthalmologist is the appropriate first specialist for acquired color vision deficiency with unknown cause. For drug-toxicity monitoring, retinal specialists experienced in OCT interpretation are best placed to perform hydroxychloroquine or ethambutol surveillance. Neurologists should co-manage cases with suspected optic neuritis, MS, or cortical lesion.
hospitalCriteria: Slit-lamp biomicroscope and dilated fundus examination capability
Validated color vision testing equipment (HRR, FM100-Hue, or Lanthony D-15d)
Spectral-domain OCT for macular and RNFL imaging
Access to automated visual field perimetry
Electrophysiology lab (ERG, VEP) for complex cases
Neuroimaging (MRI) available for optic nerve compression workup
keyFacilities: Dedicated uveitis or medical retina clinic with formal color vision testing
Neuro-ophthalmology service at academic medical centers
Occupational health ophthalmology units for toxic exposure monitoring
Glaucoma specialist clinics with serial color vision and OCT monitoring
preventionStrategies: Ensure baseline formal color vision testing before starting ethambutol, hydroxychloroquine, chloroquine, or other drugs with known retinal or optic nerve toxicity, and repeat every 6-12 months throughout treatment.
Maintain optimal IOP control in glaucoma through prescribed drops and regular specialist review to limit progressive optic nerve damage.
Achieve target HbA1c (under 7% where appropriate) and blood pressure control in diabetes to retard diabetic macular edema and preserve photoreceptor function.
Screen for nutritional deficiencies (B12, copper, folate) in at-risk populations (vegans, bariatric surgery patients, heavy alcohol users) and supplement before optic nerve symptoms develop.
Use proper engineering controls and personal protective equipment in workplaces with heavy metal or organic solvent exposure, and report color vision changes promptly to occupational health services.
lifestyleModifications: Attend all scheduled ophthalmology monitoring appointments for known at-risk conditions (glaucoma, AMD, diabetic retinopathy) — many early changes are asymptomatic.
Test each eye separately each week using a red-colored object; report any new asymmetry to the ophthalmologist promptly.
Use color-identification smartphone apps in daily and occupational settings when persistent color defects interfere with safety (e.g., electrical wiring, cooking, driving).
Inform employers in color-critical roles (aviation, maritime navigation, electrical engineering, pathology, graphic design) of any acquired deficiency so duties can be safely adjusted.
Eliminate smoking — smoking is an independent risk factor for AMD and worsens optic nerve vascular supply.
Correct nutritional deficiencies promptly with medical guidance, as optic neuropathy from deficiency is often reversible early.

recommended: Vitamin B12-rich foods (meat, fish, eggs, dairy) or fortified foods for patients at risk of deficiency-related optic neuropathy
Copper-rich foods (shellfish, nuts, seeds, legumes) to prevent copper-deficiency optic neuropathy in post-bariatric and malnourished patients
Lutein and zeaxanthin (dark leafy greens, egg yolk) — associated with lower AMD progression risk and photoreceptor protection in Age-Related Eye Disease Study 2 (AREDS2)
Omega-3 fatty acids (oily fish, walnuts) for general retinal and vascular health
avoid: Alcohol excess — contributes to nutritional deficiency optic neuropathy (tobacco-alcohol amblyopia) and worsens retinal vascular disease
Smoking — independent risk factor for AMD and retinal vascular complications
Excess zinc supplementation above 40 mg/day, which can impair copper absorption and cause copper-deficiency optic neuropathy
High-glycaemic-index foods in diabetic patients, which worsen macular edema and accelerate photoreceptor damage

exerciseGuidelines: Regular aerobic exercise improves cardiovascular health and reduces intraocular pressure by approximately 1-2 mmHg in glaucoma patients, supporting optic nerve preservation. There are no restrictions on exercise type in acquired color vision deficiency itself; contact sports are not contraindicated. Patients with associated advanced glaucoma or retinal detachment history should discuss specific restrictions with their ophthalmologist.
dailyManagement: Perform a brief monocular color comparison test each morning (view a red or orange object separately with each eye) to detect sudden change early.
Take prescribed glaucoma medications at the exact scheduled times — missed drops cause IOP spikes that damage optic nerve fibers.
Use color-identification apps before undertaking color-critical tasks (cooking, medication sorting, driving traffic lights) if persistent defects affect safety.
Attend blood glucose and blood pressure monitoring appointments as scheduled — macular edema can worsen acutely with poor metabolic control.
Carry a card or phone notification noting color deficiency for emergency settings where color coding is used in medication or equipment identification.
prognosis: Prognosis depends entirely on the underlying cause and the reversibility of the anatomical damage. Drug-induced color vision loss — particularly from ethambutol — can recover fully in 50-80% of patients if the drug is stopped within 4-8 weeks of detection; delayed cessation produces permanent optic atrophy and residual defects. Hydroxychloroquine retinopathy is largely irreversible once the bull's-eye maculopathy is detectable on OCT, which is why routine screening aims to catch the pre-symptomatic stage when the drug can still be stopped. For glaucoma, color vision stabilizes with adequate IOP control but damage already done is not recoverable. For optic neuritis, acuity typically returns to 20/20 in 90% of patients within 6 months, but residual red-green defects persist in 70-80% of affected eyes, often without causing functional disability. Nutritional optic neuropathy has the best recovery potential — partial or full color vision restoration in 40-60% of patients when supplementation is started within 6 months. When color vision loss results from irreversible photoreceptor degeneration in AMD, it is permanent and progressive.
recoveryTimeline: Ethambutol toxicity: recovery begins within 2-4 weeks of stopping the drug, may continue for 6-12 months. Optic neuritis: color defects improve over 6-12 weeks, with plateau by 6 months. Nutritional deficiency: improvement begins within 4-8 weeks of supplementation. Hydroxychloroquine maculopathy: no recovery once structural damage occurs.
complications: Permanent photoreceptor or optic nerve fiber loss if drug toxicity (ethambutol, hydroxychloroquine) is not detected early and the drug is continued.
Visual acuity loss progressing beyond color loss when macular disease or optic neuropathy advances — loss of both color and fine detail in central vision.
Occupational disability or license loss in color-critical professions (aviation, maritime, electrical, laboratory medicine) if untreated or uncompensated.
Psychological impact including anxiety and reduced quality of life, particularly in patients who relied on color in professional or artistic roles.
Delayed diagnosis of underlying systemic disease (diabetic retinopathy, glaucoma) when color loss is not recognized as a warning signal and workup is deferred.
Medication errors in patients who self-administer color-coded medications without adaptive aids, particularly in elderly patients with multiple comorbidities.

supportResources.name: Colour Blind Awareness (UK)
supportResources.description: Patient and professional resource covering both congenital and acquired color vision deficiency, workplace adjustments, and adaptive tools.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Ophthalmology — Color Blindness
supportResources.description: AAO patient-facing education on color vision deficiency diagnosis, types, and management.

supportResources.name: Prevent Blindness America
supportResources.description: Patient education, vision screening guidance, and information on eye disease monitoring for at-risk groups.

supportResources.name: RNIB (Royal National Institute of Blind People)
supportResources.description: UK-based low vision support, color vision assessment guidance, and technology recommendations for people with acquired visual impairments.

confusedWithConditions.name: Congenital achromatopsia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Congenital achromatopsia is present from birth with complete absence of cone function (total color blindness), nystagmus, photophobia, and very poor acuity. Acquired color vision deficiency develops during life, is usually partial rather than total, is often asymmetric, and is linked to a specific disease or drug. Electroretinography and history distinguish them.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acquired night blindness
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acquired night blindness (nyctalopia) involves rod photoreceptor dysfunction and manifests as difficulty seeing in dim light, with normal daytime vision and color discrimination. Acquired color vision deficiency affects cone-mediated color discrimination under normal photopic lighting. Both can coexist in retinal degenerations.

confusedWithConditions.name: Age-related macular degeneration
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: AMD causes macular photoreceptor degeneration and can produce acquired tritan color vision deficiency as a component of vision loss. Patients may initially present with color difficulties before the central drusen or neovascular membrane is clinically obvious. OCT and fundus photography confirm AMD as the underlying structural cause.

relatedConditions.name: Glaucoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Optic nerve damage from glaucoma is a leading cause of acquired red-green color deficiency; color testing is used to monitor disease progression.

relatedConditions.name: Acute iridocyclitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Severe or chronic anterior uveitis with cystoid macular edema can impair macular cone function and produce secondary color vision deficiency.

relatedConditions.name: Achromatopsia
relatedConditions.relevance: The congenital counterpart to acquired color vision deficiency; distinguished by onset at birth, complete cone dysfunction, and nystagmus.

relatedConditions.name: Diabetic retinopathy
relatedConditions.relevance: Diabetic macular edema causes the tritan-axis acquired color defect that can be an early marker of macular involvement before acuity change.

faqs.question: What is acquired color vision deficiency?
faqs.answer: Acquired color vision deficiency is a reduction in color discrimination that develops during life due to eye disease, drug toxicity, or neurological damage — distinct from congenital color blindness present since birth. It usually affects one eye more than the other and can be partially or fully reversible if the underlying cause is treated promptly.

faqs.question: What causes acquired color blindness?
faqs.answer: Common causes include glaucoma, age-related macular degeneration, diabetic retinopathy, optic neuritis, drug toxicity (ethambutol, hydroxychloroquine), nutritional deficiencies (vitamin B12, copper), and occipital cortex injury. The specific color axis affected (blue-yellow or red-green) points to the anatomical location of the damage.

faqs.question: How is acquired color vision deficiency different from congenital color blindness?
faqs.answer: Congenital color blindness is present from birth, almost always affects both eyes equally, is almost entirely a red-green defect, and is far more common in men (8% of males). Acquired deficiency develops during life, is usually asymmetric between the two eyes, often affects the blue-yellow axis first, and affects both sexes equally.

faqs.question: What drugs cause acquired color vision loss?
faqs.answer: Ethambutol (used for tuberculosis), hydroxychloroquine and chloroquine (used for rheumatoid arthritis and lupus), sildenafil and other PDE5 inhibitors, digitalis, amiodarone at high doses, and cisplatin can all impair color vision. Regular monitoring is standard care for patients on the first two drugs.

faqs.question: Can acquired color vision deficiency be reversed?
faqs.answer: Reversibility depends on the cause. Ethambutol toxicity reverses in 50-80% of patients when the drug is stopped within 4-8 weeks. Vitamin B12 deficiency optic neuropathy often recovers with supplementation. Hydroxychloroquine maculopathy and glaucoma optic atrophy are largely irreversible once structural damage is established.

faqs.question: How is acquired color vision deficiency diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Formal testing with pseudoisochromatic plates (Ishihara, Hardy-Rand-Rittler) screens for the defect axis; the Farnsworth-Munsell 100-Hue test or Lanthony D-15d quantifies severity and direction. OCT, visual field testing, and electrophysiology identify the structural cause.

faqs.question: Is blue-yellow color confusion more worrying than red-green?
faqs.answer: Acquired blue-yellow (tritan-axis) defects are often the earliest warning sign of macular disease — age-related macular degeneration, diabetic macular edema, or hydroxychloroquine toxicity — making them clinically important. Red-green acquired defects typically indicate optic nerve disease such as glaucoma or optic neuritis.

faqs.question: Can glaucoma cause color vision loss?
faqs.answer: Yes. Glaucoma damages the optic nerve fibers that carry color signals, producing a red-green type acquired color defect. Color vision testing can detect functional loss before standard visual field tests show a deficit, making it a useful supplementary monitoring tool in early glaucoma.

faqs.question: Does optic neuritis cause color vision problems?
faqs.answer: Yes. Optic neuritis causes red-green color desaturation in the affected eye — the red lens test (comparing the brightness of a red object between eyes) is a sensitive bedside check. Residual red-green defects persist in 70-80% of eyes after optic neuritis even when visual acuity returns to normal.

faqs.question: Do color-enhancing glasses help acquired color vision deficiency?
faqs.answer: Specialized tinted lenses (ChromaGen, X-Chrom) and color-filter glasses improve color task performance in some patients, with improved Ishihara pass rates in 60-80% of users in small trials. They do not restore normal color perception but reduce practical errors in color-critical activities.

faqs.question: Can smartphones help people with color vision deficiency?
faqs.answer: Yes. Apps such as Color Blind Pal, Microsoft Seeing AI, and EnChroma's assistive tools identify and name colors from the camera in real time. Operating system accessibility filters (iOS Color Filters, Android Color Correction) can also shift color presentation to a more distinguishable range.

faqs.question: Should I be screened for color vision loss if I take hydroxychloroquine?
faqs.answer: Yes. AAO 2016 guidelines recommend a baseline examination within one year of starting hydroxychloroquine, then annual screening from year 5 (or earlier in high-risk patients). Screening includes spectral-domain OCT and multifocal ERG or fundus autofluorescence; formal color testing is part of the full assessment.

faqs.question: Does diabetes cause color vision problems?
faqs.answer: Yes. Diabetic macular edema disrupts the short-wave (blue) cone photoreceptors and produces acquired tritan-axis color deficiency. This can occur before visual acuity declines, making color testing a useful early functional marker. Anti-VEGF treatment of macular edema can improve color contrast sensitivity.

faqs.question: Can vitamin B12 deficiency affect color vision?
faqs.answer: Yes. Severe vitamin B12 deficiency causes optic neuropathy with progressive color vision loss alongside reduced central acuity. It is seen in vegans, patients post-bariatric surgery, and those on long-term metformin or antacids. Intramuscular B12 supplementation can restore color vision if started early.

faqs.question: Is color vision testing used in occupational health?
faqs.answer: Yes. Aviation, maritime, railway, electrical engineering, pathology, military, and many industrial roles require satisfactory color vision for safety. Acquired defects must be reported to employers and licensing bodies in regulated professions. Formal annual testing is required in some high-risk occupations.

regionalNames.name: Deficiencia adquirida de la visión del color

metaTitle: Acquired Color Vision Deficiency: Causes & Treatment
metaDescription: Acquired color vision deficiency: color loss from eye disease, drug toxicity or nerve damage. Tritan vs red-green defects, diagnosis and treatment reviewed.
